risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned to either the TM or health education (HE) arms using a stratified block design... Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician" Comment: This describes a proper randomization process with stratification and block design, and the biostatistician performed the allocation, which is sufficient to support a truly random allocation sequence.

_evidence.method_quote: Subjects were randomly assigned to either the TM or health education (HE) arms using a stratified block design. The strata were gender (M/F), age (above and below median for each cohort), and lipid-lowering medication (yes/no). Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly assigned using a stratified block design with gender, age, and lipid-lowering medication strata.
context_clues: The strata were gender (M/F), age (above and below median for each cohort), and lipid-lowering medication (yes/no)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator." Comment: This is an explicit concealment mechanism. The article states that the allocation schedule was concealed from those enrolling participants, which indicates the recruiter could not foresee the next assignment before enrollment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial." Comment: The paper explicitly states that this was single-blinded and that double blinding was not feasible, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded. Because the study assessed multiple subjective/self-reported outcomes (e.g. exercise, alcohol, cigarettes, anger, depression, hostility) as well as objective outcomes, knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect at least the subjective outcomes; therefore the dominant risk for this domain is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Investigators, data collectors and data management staff were blinded to group assignment. Intervention groups met separately to minimize contamination. Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial.
_evidence.blinding_method: The control intervention was a cardiovascular health education program designed to match the format of the experimental intervention for instructional time, instructor attention, participant expectancy, social support, and other nonspecific factors.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Investigators, data collectors and data management staff were blinded to group assignment... All clinical end points were adjudicated by a blinded, independent reviewer who applied standardized and validated criteria." Comment: Outcome assessment for the major clinical endpoints was explicitly blinded, and those endpoints were objective (mortality, MI, stroke) and adjudicated independently. The additional intermediate outcomes reported (BP, BMI, lifestyle behaviors, psychosocial scales) were collected by study staff described as blinded, so there is no positive evidence of unblinded outcome assessment for the reported outcomes.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Investigators, data collectors and data management staff were blinded to group assignment. Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial. All clinical end points were adjudicated by a blinded, independent reviewer who applied standardized and validated criteria.

outcomes.label: primary clinical end point (all-cause mortality, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke)
secondary clinical end point (cardiovascular mortality, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, coronary revascularization, hospitalization for ischemic heart disease-non-MI or heart failure)
attendance at group instructional and follow-up meetings

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All primary and secondary outcomes were analyzed using the intention-to-treat principle." Comment: The primary survival analysis included all randomized subjects, and the paper reports per-arm randomized vs analysed numbers for the main outcome (TM 99/99, HE 102/102) with no post-randomization loss on the analysed primary endpoint. Although 58 subjects did not participate in phase 2 re-enrollment, the authors state that mortality data were still collected for all subjects regardless of re-enrollment status and sensitivity analyses including these events gave similar results, so this does not indicate problematic attrition for the primary outcome.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “TM (n = 99)” randomized; “The rate of nonparticipation in the treatment groups was 19/99 or 19% in the TM group” and “At the beginning of phase 2, 143 subjects were re-enrolled in the second phase. Fifty-eight subjects from phase 1 did not participate in phase 2 because of death, attrition or lack of informed consent.”

“HE (n =102)” randomized; “The rate of nonparticipation in the treatment groups was ... 10/102 or 10% in the HE group” and “At the beginning of phase 2, 143 subjects were re-enrolled in the second phase. Fifty-eight subjects from phase 1 did not participate in phase 2 because of death, attrition or lack of informed consent.”

_evidence.notes: Per-arm losses are described for phase 2 re-enrollment/nonparticipation rather than as a complete CONSORT attrition table for the primary outcome; the primary survival analysis included all randomized subjects with censoring, and the paper states that “All primary and secondary outcomes were analyzed using the intention-to-treat principle.”
